journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal for Equity in Health

title-group.article-title: Community participation in responding to health misinformation

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/020x1hr04grid.500021.6Community Health And Information Network (CHAIN), Kampala, Uganda
2Asia Pacific Oncology Alliance, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Responses to health misinformation increasingly emphasize the importance of community engagement, yet the role of participation and power sharing remains under-theorized and inconsistently described in the peer-reviewed literature. Drawing on findings from the systematic review Overcoming Health Misinformation in Marginalized Groups, which examined 32 studies evaluating approaches to tackling health misinformation, we reflect on how the depth and quality of community participation shape trust, engagement, and intervention success. We argue that sustained partnership, shared decision-making, and local ownership enhance message credibility and reach by embedding health information within trusted social contexts. We propose strengthening equity-focused responses through structured reporting tools that center power, trust, and co-production as practical design principles rather than implementation add-ons. Making participation visible is critical if community engagement is to move beyond rhetoric and contribute meaningfully to addressing health misinformation in contexts of low institutional trust.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Trust and relational dynamics in health misinformation
sec.p: Health misinformation is increasingly recognized as a central concern in global health, shaping health beliefs, intentions, and behaviors across diverse populations. Consistent with prior work, we define health misinformation as false, inaccurate, or misleading health-related information that is shared without an intent to deceive [1, 2]. Many interventions aimed at countering misinformation, such as fact-checking, fall short of addressing the factors that shape why people believe, share, or reject certain information. Such approaches are grounded in the “information deficit model” [3], which assumes that simply providing accurate information will correct misconceptions and alter beliefs and behaviors. In reality, the causal chain from information exposure to health beliefs and behavior change is complex and context-dependent, shaped by social identity, institutional trust, and prior experience [4–7].

Three related but distinct concepts are relevant in the context of health misinformation: health information (message content), health communication (the processes through which information is framed and exchanged), and health literacy (the capacity to access, interpret, and use information) [8–10]. Recent health literacy literature emphasizes that a person’s ability to access, interpret, and use health information is shaped by institutional practices, communicative demands, and power relations, rather than by individual skills or knowledge alone [11, 12]. These factors may also drive how individuals interact with health misinformation, particularly in communities shaped by structural marginalization. Historical exclusion, discrimination, and unequal access to health and social resources can erode trust in institutions responsible for producing and disseminating health information [13, 14]. In such contexts, official messages may be perceived as misaligned with lived experience, culturally irrelevant, or lacking legitimacy. Mistrust may increase reliance on informal networks or alternative information sources, where health misinformation can circulate more readily [15, 16]. Importantly, these dynamics shape not only exposure to health misinformation but also how health claims are interpreted, evaluated, and acted upon [17, 18].

As leaders in patient advocacy working with marginalized communities in Africa, Asia, and beyond for more than 20 years, we have firsthand experience of how top-down approaches to overcoming health misinformation have failed to gain traction because they are not rooted in, or informed by, beliefs and realities on the ground. We have also seen the power of patient involvement in designing and delivering accurate health information. However, we have found gaps in how these viewpoints are represented in the published literature.

sec.title: Contextualizing findings from a systematic review of the literature
sec.p: A recent systematic review, Overcoming Health Misinformation in Marginalized Groups [19] examined 32 studies evaluating misinformation interventions in marginalized communities. Across studies, conventional notions of who qualifies as an “expert” were challenged, with credibility most often attributed to messengers who shared the community’s language, culture, and lived experience rather than to institutional authorities. Peer educators, youth leaders, and faith-based messengers emerged as particularly trusted voices, bridging formal knowledge with local understanding. There was also evidence that cultural and linguistic concordance between messenger and community enhanced perceived relevance and acceptance of health messages.

Several studies in the review described some form of community participation or co-production as a factor contributing to their success (Fig. 1). Examples included community-partnered vaccination campaigns using peer storytelling and bilingual outreach to strengthen trust and vaccine confidence among Latinx communities in California [20]; co-designed culturally grounded COVID-19 education and outreach, delivered by community health promoters in Latinx and Indigenous communities [21]; multilingual health information materials with cultural mediators, co-created with refugee, migrant and indigenous groups [22, 23]; and door-to-door engagement in Mexico to identify and counter misinformation following the WHO risk communication guidance for COVID-19 [24]. Co-produced approaches act on key health communication processes, such as messenger credibility, narrative alignment and pathways of information circulation, and may support situated forms of health literacy that develop within trusted relationships. In contexts characterized by low institutional trust, these mechanisms help to explain why engagement-based interventions may be particularly important for addressing misinformation, as they operate on how health claims are evaluated and acted upon rather than relying solely on corrective or top-down messaging.

caption.p: Types of community involvement identified in misinformation response interventions

sec.p: Despite widespread endorsement of community participation as a principle, the quality, depth and influence of participation were only minimally described in the reviewed studies [19]. Many reports described engagement taking the form of one-off consultations with no mechanisms for follow-up; data collection without the community’s involvement in dissemination; and little or no evidence of opportunities for shared decision-making. Structured reporting tools, such as the Checklist for Inclusive Community Involvement in Health Research (CHICO) [25] or Pratt’s (2021) Sharing Power with Communities toolkit [26] were not used in any studies included in the review. Partial or limited forms of engagement may not only fail to build trust but can inadvertently undermine it, particularly when communities perceive their role as procedural rather than meaningful [27]. In misinformation contexts, these risks may be particularly salient, as they can reinforce skepticism toward official health messages and increase openness to alternative sources perceived as more authentic or community-aligned, even when those sources circulate misleading information [28, 29].

sec.title: A call to increase depth, quality and transparency of community participation
sec.p: Tackling health misinformation is not solely a matter of correcting inaccurate claims; it also requires rethinking the relationships between researchers, practitioners, and the communities they seek to support. Community participation has the potential to transform health communication from a process conducted for communities into one created with them, ensuring that interventions reflect lived experiences and are contextually grounded. When communities are meaningfully involved in co-authoring solutions, they are better positioned to influence how contested claims are interpreted, whether information is trusted, and how messages circulate within social networks, thereby shaping the relevance and acceptance of interventions. Practical, structured frameworks, such as CHICO, can support this shift by guiding teams to reflect on power dynamics, assess partnership strengths, plan collaborative activities that genuinely involve community members, and report these in a way that helps others to learn from challenges and replicate success. Rather than treating engagement as an implementation add-on, they position community partnership as a core design principle for effective and equitable responses to health misinformation. Journals and funders might consider encouraging clearer reporting of governance structures and decision rights, particularly in misinformation research, where trust and legitimacy are crucial mediators.

sec.title: About the authors
sec.p: Regina Mariam Namata Kamoga is Executive Director of Community Health and Information Network (CHAIN) in Uganda and founding director of World Patient Alliance. CHAIN promotes empowerment of people living with and affected by HIV, TB, malaria, and non-communicable diseases, while supporting vulnerable children and advancing health literacy, patient-centered care, and patient safety. She is a WHO Patient Safety Champion, member of WHO’s Patients for Patient Safety advisory group as well as a board member of The Ugandan Academy of Health Innovation and Impact.

Ranjit Kaur Pritam Singh is a patient advocate and consultant to National Cancer Society of Malaysia, and, she previously served as President of Breast Cancer Welfare Association Malaysia. She serves on multiple international boards including Asia-Pacific Oncology Alliance, Reach to Recovery International, and Together Against Cancer Association Malaysia. She conducts training in peer support, peer navigation in cancer care, doctor-patient communication skills, and health literacy for medical students and healthcare professionals.

Emma Andrews is Pfizer’s Vice President, U.S. Business Unit Policy and Public Affairs and Patient Advocacy, leading the organization’s work to embed the patient perspective across all Pfizer’s efforts by working with internal and external stakeholders to advance patient-focused programs across the enterprise and to deliver outcomes that matter to patients and those involved in their care. Her focus areas have spanned infectious diseases, neglected tropical diseases, malaria and women’s health. Emma’s experience growing up in Uganda and Cote d’Ivoire has shaped her commitment to reducing healthcare disparities, improving health literacy, and advancing health equity in all she does.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: Writing and editorial support was provided by Studio Health: Andreea Prisecaru, Candida Halton, Millie McLuskie and Veronika Rysinova.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: All authors conceptualized and reviewed the manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This article was funded by Pfizer Inc.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: RKPS has received consultancy fees from Pfizer Inc, but has not received fees for the work associated with this article. RMNK has received consultancy fees from Pfizer Inc, but has not received fees for the work associated with this article. EA is an employee of Pfizer Inc., and owns shares/stock options in Pfizer Inc. SS is an employee of Studio Health, which has received consultancy fees from Pfizer Inc.
